topic: An employee finds out a junior colleague makes more money
raw_output:
[story 1]

The fluorescent lights of the office hummed with an oppressive monotony, mirroring the dull ache in Elias’s chest. He stared at the screen, the spreadsheet detailing quarterly allocations shimmering mockingly. When he finally cross-referenced the data, the numbers swam before his eyes, and the reality hit him like a physical blow. Sarah, the new hire, was earning nearly double what he did for the same workload. A cold, heavy sensation settled in his stomach, tightening his throat until swallowing felt like sandpaper. He traced a line on the paper with a trembling finger, the ink blurring. He felt a sudden, intense urge to retreat, to simply dissolve into the beige cubicle wall. Every small task he performed suddenly felt insignificant, a mere flicker against the bright, undeniable reality of Sarah’s compensation. He picked up his mug, the ceramic warm against his palm, and let it slip, the sound echoing too loudly in the quiet space.

[story 2]

“Another meeting, another pointless update,” Mark muttered under his breath, the sound barely audible above the keyboard clicking. He kept glancing at the framed photo on his desk—a picture of him smiling, a smile that felt increasingly like a performance. When he saw the pay stub comparison, the air seemed to thin out. His hands started to shake, the tremor running up his forearms. It wasn't just the money; it was the implication—that his effort, his dedication, was somehow miscalculated, undervalued. He pushed his chair back abruptly, the scrape of metal on linoleum sharp and accusatory. He stood up, needing space, needing to escape the suffocating closeness of the open-plan office. He walked toward the window, watching the city rush by, feeling utterly detached, as if he were observing a life that wasn't his own.

[story 3]

“I just… I don't understand,” whispered David, his voice thin and reedy as he reread the figures for the third time. The numbers didn't lie; they were stark, undeniable proof of a disparity he couldn't reconcile. A wave of profound emptiness washed over him, leaving him feeling strangely numb and heavy, as if his limbs were filled with lead. He looked down at his hands; they seemed foreign, clumsy instruments incapable of grasping anything substantial. He tried to focus on the task at hand, but the words swam, refusing to form coherent thoughts. He started meticulously organizing his desk, aligning the pens and staplers with obsessive precision, a desperate attempt to impose order on a world that had suddenly tilted on its axis, leaving him stranded on the wrong side of the ledger.
